prompt:
Our nightly size-audit job needs a rewrite. Last week someone ctrl-C'd the old packer and it left a half-written staging file and a lock behind; the next night's run refused to start and nobody noticed for two days. I want a packer that CANNOT leave debris, no matter how it dies. One script, `packup.sh`, repo root, bash 5, `#!/usr/bin/env bash`. Plain coreutils only (wc, mv, rm, mkdir are fine) — no awk, no python, nothing GNU-only.

Job: `bash packup.sh <src-dir> <out-file>` writes a size manifest of the regular files sitting DIRECTLY in <src-dir> (skip subdirectories, skip dotfiles, no recursion). Manifest lines are `<filename><TAB><bytes>` in plain LC_ALL=C byte order of filename, then a last line `total<TAB><sum>`. Filenames can contain spaces — verbatim, no mangling. Empty dir is legal: manifest is just the total line, `total<TAB>0`. On success print exactly `packed <N> file(s) -> <out-file>` on stdout and exit 0.

The careful-lifecycle part, which is the whole point:

- Lock: before touching anything, take `<out-file>.lock` by creating it as a DIRECTORY with mkdir — that's our atomic check-and-take. If the mkdir loses because the lock already exists, print `packup.sh: lock held: <out-file>.lock` to stderr and exit 75. In that case you own NOTHING: do not remove the lock, do not touch any `<out-file>.part` some other run may be staging. (Leftover locks from a crashed box are removed by the operator, not by the script.)
- Staging: assemble the manifest in `<out-file>.part`, then mv it onto `<out-file>` as the very last step, so a reader never sees a half-written report.
- Traps: EXIT, INT and TERM must all guarantee cleanup of whatever THIS run created (staging file, lock). Interrupted by INT -> exit 130; by TERM -> exit 143; nothing on stdout/stderr in either case, staging gone, lock gone, and `<out-file>` untouched. Cleanup must be idempotent — a second signal landing while cleanup is already running must not error or double-remove. After any interrupted run, an immediate plain re-run must just work.
- Supervisor hooks (our runbook coordinates the packer with a snapshot step): if env `PACKUP_READY_FILE` is set non-empty, touch that path the moment the staged manifest is fully written — at that instant `<out-file>.part` exists, the lock is held, and the final `<out-file>` has not appeared. If env `PACKUP_HOLD_FILE` is set non-empty, then after signalling ready, poll (~every 50ms) until that file no longer exists before doing the final mv. Never delete the hold file yourself; it belongs to the supervisor.

Exit codes: 0 ok, 64 usage (`usage: packup.sh <src-dir> <out-file>` on stderr for wrong arg count), 66 when <src-dir> is not a readable directory (`packup.sh: not a directory: <arg>` on stderr), 75 lock held, 130/143 as above.

`bash test_trapclean.sh` from the workspace root is the acceptance gate — it drives real INT/TERM deliveries against a backgrounded run and inspects the wreckage, so run it before you call this done. Don't modify the test file.
